metrics.title: Liquefaction Capacity
metrics.label: Largest US LNG Exporter — ~50% US / ~11% Global Supply

metrics.title: Contracted Volume
metrics.label: 15–20 Year Take-or-Pay SPAs — ~15-Year Weighted Avg Remaining Life

moat.description: Cheniere's moat is the only at-scale, fully operational US LNG export platform — Sabine Pass and Corpus Christi together produce ~52 MTPA, with ~95% of capacity contracted under 15–20 year take-or-pay SPAs that pay a fixed liquefaction fee regardless of whether cargo is lifted, layered onto FERC, DOE, and Coast Guard licensing barriers that take 5–10 years and tens of billions to replicate.
moat.analysisSummary: Cheniere sits at the intersection of **three structural tailwinds** — global gas demand growth, European energy diversification away from Russia, and AI-driven gas-fired data center power demand — protected by a moat built on physically irreplicable export terminals, multi-agency regulatory licensing, and 20-year fixed-fee contracts that convert it from a commodity producer into an infrastructure tolling business:

analysisPoints.title: Take-or-Pay Contracts Convert LNG into Toll-Road Cash Flows
analysisPoints.text: Cheniere sells ~95% of its LNG capacity under 15–20 year Sale and Purchase Agreements (SPAs) and IPM agreements with creditworthy utility and trading counterparties globally (Galp, CPC, Total, BP, Shell, BG, Equinor, and others). The contract structure has two layers: a fixed liquefaction fee around $2.84/MMBtu paid regardless of whether cargo is lifted, plus a variable fee at 115% of Henry Hub covering fuel and shipping. The fixed fee alone covers the company's full cost stack, debt service, and dividend — meaning Cheniere's downside is structurally insulated from spot LNG prices for the next 15+ years on a weighted-average basis. Run-rate distributable cash flow is guided to ~$30/share by 2030 after full deployment of buyback authorizations.

analysisPoints.title: Multi-Agency Regulatory Lock-In with 5–10 Year Replication Cost
analysisPoints.text: Operating a US LNG export terminal requires FERC siting authorization, DOE non-FTA export approval, Coast Guard MARSEC waterway suitability assessment, EPA air permits, and state-level coastal zone approvals — collectively a 5–10 year permitting process that has cost competitors $3–6B per project. The Biden-era DOE pause on non-FTA export approvals slowed new entrants for over a year; while the Trump administration is now actively fast-tracking new approvals (FERC issued a Notice of Inquiry in November 2025 to streamline LNG facility approvals), Cheniere's existing 52 MTPA platform is already grandfathered, and its expansion projects (CCL Stage 3, CCL Trains 8&9, SPL Expansion) are already permitted or in advanced review. New competitors must spend most of a decade catching up.

analysisPoints.title: Sabine Pass + Corpus Christi: Physically Irreplicable Operating Platforms
analysisPoints.text: Sabine Pass (LA) is a 6-train, ~30 MTPA platform — the first and largest US LNG export facility ever built. Corpus Christi (TX) operates 3 large trains plus the Stage 3 midscale facility (first LNG achieved 2025; remaining trains complete in 2026), with Trains 8 & 9 under construction targeting 2028. Combined run-rate capacity is increasing by over 10% to 60+ MTPA inclusive of Stage 3, Trains 8&9, and identified debottlenecking — with line of sight to 90+ MTPA across future brownfield expansions on existing sites. These integrated terminals (deepwater berths, gas pipeline interconnects, storage tanks, multi-train liquefaction) cannot be replicated except by building entirely new sites; Venture Global's CP2 (FID March 2026, ~20 MTPA) is the only credible US challenger and remains 2–3 years from first cargo.

growth.description: 2026 guidance midpoints of $7.0B Adj. EBITDA and $4.6B DCF (including a one-time alt fuel tax credit of $300M+ in Q1) sit roughly flat vs FY2025 ($6.9B / $5.3B) as new Stage 3 trains ramp; underlying volumetric growth is 51–53 MTPA in 2026, climbing to 60+ MTPA run-rate by ~2028 as Trains 8&9 come online and SPL/CCL brownfield expansion FIDs land in 2026–2027. Per-share DCF growth — guided to ~$30 run-rate by 2030 from ~$20 in 2025 — is accelerated by the new share repurchase authorization announced February 2026 that completed the '20/20 Vision' capital plan ahead of schedule.

additionalNote.title: Growth Drivers (3-Year Horizon)
points: CCL Stage 3 ramp: first LNG achieved 2025; remaining 3 of 7 midscale trains complete in 2026, adding ~10 MTPA of high-margin contracted capacity that flows directly to 2027 EBITDA and DCF
CCL Trains 8 & 9 (FID closed 2025, ~3 MTPA): groundwork and piling for Train 8 completed; substantial completion forecast for 2028 — adds incremental contracted volume at attractive build cost

SPL Expansion + future CCL brownfield FIDs targeted 2026–2027: Cheniere has line of sight to 90+ MTPA total platform capacity, with brownfield economics far superior to greenfield competitors (existing pipeline, port, and utility infrastructure)

Capital return flywheel: $30/share run-rate DCF target by 2030 implies a 25–30% per-share DCF CAGR through buybacks — at current share count of ~210M, the new repurchase authorization can compound the per-share value even with flat aggregate cash flow

AI/data center gas demand: hyperscaler power buildouts in PJM, ERCOT, and Southeast US drive incremental gas demand, supporting Henry Hub prices and creating Asian/European LNG buyer urgency to lock in long-term US offtake

growthAnalysis.scoreDerivation: Base 67 (8–15% blended CAGR) + 5 contracted/recurring (95% take-or-pay) + 5 TAM expansion (global LNG demand + AI gas) + 0 margin (stable, Stage 3 accretive) = 77

drivers.name: Liquefaction Volume
drivers.metric: 51–53 MTPA in 2026 ramping to 60+ MTPA run-rate ~2028

drivers.name: Brownfield Expansion FIDs
drivers.metric: Line of sight to 90+ MTPA; SPL/CCL expansion FIDs targeted 2026–2027

growthAnalysis.keyRisk: If Venture Global's CP2 (20 MTPA, FID March 2026) ramps faster than guided and pulls SPA pricing down >15% by 2028, Cheniere's brownfield expansion economics compress and the per-share DCF target slips beyond 2030

valuation.description: At ~$274, LNG trades around 22× consensus 2026 EPS of ~$12.33 and ~12× 2026 DCF — a meaningful discount to contracted infrastructure peers (toll roads, data center REITs, regulated pipelines all 15–20× DCF). The mean analyst price target is $295 (range $259–$340); the stock sits about 12% below the $310 base case and offers ~53% upside to the $420 bull case. FCF yield is ~8% in 2026, rising as Stage 3 trains complete.

valuationNote.text: Cheniere is structurally mispriced as a commodity gas producer when its cash flow profile is closer to a regulated pipeline or contracted infrastructure asset. With ~95% of capacity locked under 15-year weighted-avg take-or-pay SPAs, the appropriate frame is 12–15× run-rate DCF: $30/share run-rate by 2030 → $360–$450 fair value range. The current 9× run-rate DCF multiple reflects residual concerns about Venture Global supply growth and Henry Hub/JKM spread compression, but the take-or-pay structure means those risks impact marketing margin (a small fraction of total cash flow) rather than the contracted base.

peAnalysis.summary: Forward P/E of ~22× is roughly in line with the S&P 500 and at a premium to traditional E&P peers (~10–14×) but a clear discount to contracted-infrastructure comparables (15–20×). PEG of ~2.0× looks expensive on a pure earnings-growth lens, but the right multiple is Price/DCF where ~12× current and ~9× run-rate-2030 imply substantial re-rating room as the market accepts the toll-road characterization. The trailing-to-forward P/E gap is narrow because 2025 already captured most of the post-Russia LNG repricing — growth from here is volumetric (Stage 3 + Trains 8&9) rather than spread-driven.

bear.description: Venture Global's CP2 floods the global LNG market by 2028, Henry Hub/JKM spreads compress structurally, brownfield expansion economics deteriorate, and Cheniere's brownfield FIDs are delayed — multiple compresses to 7× DCF on the contracted base.
points: Venture Global CP2 (20 MTPA) ramps faster than guided and exits the construction-cost overrun risk; combined with ~15 MTPA of Qatar expansion volumes coming online in 2027, global LNG enters oversupply and JKM-Henry Hub spreads compress to <$2/MMBtu, pressuring marketing margin and uncontracted volume realizations

SPL Expansion and CCL brownfield FIDs slip into 2027–2028 amid weak counterparty appetite for new 20-year offtake at fixed-fee levels Cheniere requires; the 90+ MTPA long-term capacity vision becomes 70 MTPA by mid-2030s, reducing the long-term DCF compounding profile

Run-rate 2030 DCF/share holds at ~$25 instead of the guided $30, and the market re-rates the stock to 8× run-rate DCF reflecting commodity-generator concerns rather than infrastructure status — implying $200/share at ~210M shares

base.description: Stage 3 trains complete on schedule in 2026, Trains 8 & 9 substantial completion 2028, SPL and CCL brownfield expansion FIDs land in 2026–2027 at attractive contract pricing, and per-share DCF reaches $30 by 2030 with the buyback authorization fully deployed.
points: Remaining 3 Stage 3 trains complete commissioning in 2026 as guided; combined with debottlenecking, run-rate capacity reaches 60+ MTPA by 2028, supporting EBITDA growth from $7B (2026) to $8.5B+ by 2028

SPL Expansion (positive FID 2026 or 2027) and CCL brownfield FIDs add visible growth runway through end of decade; contract pricing on new SPAs holds at fixed-fee levels supporting expansion economics

Buyback authorization is deployed at ~$280–$310 average levels, reducing share count from ~210M to ~180M by 2030 and accelerating per-share DCF to the $30 run-rate target; market re-rates to 12× run-rate DCF, implying ~$310 base case price target

bull.description: AI/data center gas demand drives Henry Hub above $5/MMBtu structurally, Asian and European utilities pre-buy aggressive long-dated SPAs, and Cheniere's full 90+ MTPA platform vision FIDs by 2028 — the market re-rates LNG as a contracted infrastructure asset at 15× run-rate DCF.
points: AI gas demand pulls Henry Hub structurally above $5/MMBtu through the late 2020s, supporting JKM-Henry Hub spreads above $4/MMBtu — Cheniere's marketing segment captures a windfall on the ~5% uncontracted volumes and signs new SPAs at premium fixed-fee tiers

Asian/European geopolitical events (further Russia sanctions, Middle East disruption, Iran-Qatar gas flow risk already showing up in 2026) drive utility customers to lock in 20-year fixed-fee LNG aggressively, and Cheniere FIDs the full 90+ MTPA pipeline by 2028 with attractive returns

Per-share DCF reaches $35+ by 2030 as buybacks compound at lower share count and brownfield expansions accrete; market re-rates to 15× run-rate DCF (matching toll-road comps), implying ~$420 share price

learnedInterfaces.note: N/A — Cheniere is a B2B LNG export operator with no consumer-facing UI; SPA counterparties interact via long-form contracts and operational scheduling, not interfaces that create lock-in.

businessLogic.note: N/A — Cheniere does not sell embedded software or proprietary business-process logic; this moat category does not apply to its physical-asset business model.

publicDataAccess.note: N/A — Cheniere does not control access to a unique public data set; gas market and Henry Hub data are public commodity information.

talentScarcity.note: LNG terminal operations require specialized engineering (cryogenic processing, marine terminal logistics, vessel scheduling, FERC compliance) that takes years to develop; Cheniere is the largest US employer of LNG operations talent and benefits from the steepest learning curve, but the global LNG industry is expanding (Qatar, Australia, Mozambique) and the talent pool is gradually growing.

bundling.note: N/A — Cheniere sells a single product (liquefied natural gas delivered FOB or DES) with optional shipping logistics; there is no multi-product bundle whose integration creates additional value.

proprietaryData.note: N/A — Cheniere holds operational data on its own plants but no externally-valuable proprietary dataset; this moat category does not meaningfully apply.

regulatoryLockIn.note: Operating a US LNG export terminal requires FERC siting authorization, DOE non-FTA export approval, US Coast Guard MARSEC waterway suitability, EPA air permits, and state coastal zone approvals — a 5–10 year multi-agency process costing $3–6B per project. Cheniere's existing 52 MTPA platform is grandfathered under the most stringent permitting era; expansion FIDs build incrementally on already-permitted sites, while greenfield competitors must restart the entire stack.

networkEffects.note: N/A — LNG is a commodity delivered to global gas grids; additional buyers do not increase the value of Cheniere's service to existing buyers, and there is no two-sided network effect.

transactionEmbedding.note: ~95% of Cheniere's capacity is contracted under 15–20 year take-or-pay SPAs with global utility and trading counterparties (Galp, CPC, Total, BP, Shell, BG, Equinor, and others); weighted-average remaining contract life is ~15 years. Counterparties have integrated Cheniere offtake into long-term gas procurement strategies and downstream fuel-supply commitments — unwinding requires alternative LNG suppliers at scale, which globally remain capacity-constrained.

systemOfRecord.note: N/A — Cheniere is not a system of record for any information function; this moat category does not apply to a physical LNG export business.

tenMoats.verdict: Cheniere is a clear net beneficiary of AI: hyperscaler data center buildouts in the US drive structural Henry Hub support and gas-fired generation demand, while Asian and European utilities accelerate long-term LNG procurement to back AI-era power needs. AI cannot replicate FERC/DOE-permitted terminals, replace LNG vessels, or shortcut the 5–10 year multi-agency permitting stack — the regulatory lock-in and contracted SPA portfolio are entirely AI-immune. The principal AI-related risk is highly efficient inference reducing data center power demand growth more than expected, but even in that case the 95% contracted base preserves cash flow.
